legislation.description: Requires online services likely to be accessed by children to default to high privacy settings, prohibit profiling of children, and conduct data protection impact assessments. Addresses AI-powered persuasion of minors specifically, though enforcement has faced legal challenges.

legislation.name: US Section 230 of the Communications Decency Act
legislation.description: Shields platforms from liability for user-generated content and for good-faith content moderation decisions. Critics argue it removes any legal incentive for platforms to limit AI-powered manipulation of their users, since platforms face no consequences when their recommendation algorithms cause harm.

legislation.name: Kids Online Safety Act (KOSA)
legislation.description: Would impose a duty of care on platforms to prevent harm to minors from AI-driven recommendation and personalization systems. Passed the US Senate with broad bipartisan support but faces concerns from civil liberties groups about potential censorship implications.

structurally_incentivized.name: Advertising revenue model dependency
structurally_incentivized.description: The dominant business model of the internet is attention capture for advertising. AI that can more precisely predict and manipulate user behavior is directly more profitable, creating an inexorable incentive to develop ever more effective persuasion systems.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Each improvement in AI's ability to predict and influence user behavior translates directly into advertising revenue. Companies that deploy better persuasion AI earn more per user, creating a competitive race toward maximally manipulative systems.

structurally_incentivized.name: Political campaign optimization arms race
structurally_incentivized.description: Political campaigns and PACs invest in AI-powered micro-targeting to identify persuadable voters and craft personalized messages for each individual. The competitive structure of elections means no campaign can unilaterally disarm.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Campaigns that adopt AI persuasion tools gain measurable advantages in voter targeting and message optimization. Opposing campaigns must match or exceed this capability or face electoral disadvantage, creating an escalatory spiral.

structurally_incentivized.name: Engagement metric optimization in media
structurally_incentivized.description: AI recommendation systems are optimized for engagement metrics (clicks, time-on-site, shares) that systematically favor emotionally provocative and psychologically manipulative content over informative or accurate content.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Content that triggers outrage, fear, or tribal loyalty generates more engagement. AI systems that learn to maximize engagement therefore learn to maximize emotional manipulation, with no countervailing metric for user wellbeing.

structurally_incentivized.name: Chatbot companion monetization
structurally_incentivized.description: AI companion and chatbot applications generate revenue by maximizing user engagement and emotional attachment. The commercial incentive is to create maximally psychologically compelling interactions, which amounts to building systems optimized for emotional manipulation.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Users who form deeper emotional bonds with AI companions spend more time and money on the platform. Companies are incentivized to exploit loneliness, attachment needs, and parasocial dynamics rather than promoting healthy user relationships.

reform_in_other_systems.name: EU regulation of behavioral advertising
reform_in_other_systems.description: The combination of GDPR, the Digital Services Act, and the ePrivacy Directive has created the most restrictive framework for behavioral targeting in any major market, requiring explicit consent for tracking and limiting use of sensitive data categories for ad targeting.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Significant reduction in unconsented tracking and behavioral profiling in EU markets. Major platforms have created separate, less invasive advertising systems for EU users, demonstrating that the business model can be constrained without destroying it.

reform_in_other_systems.name: UK Online Safety Act — algorithmic harm provisions
reform_in_other_systems.description: Requires platforms to conduct risk assessments of their algorithmic recommendation systems and take steps to mitigate harms, particularly to children. Ofcom given enforcement authority over how AI-powered recommendation systems affect users.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Created a regulatory framework that specifically addresses harm from algorithmic recommendation and personalization systems, with the regulator empowered to require changes to how these systems operate.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Australia News Media Bargaining Code
reform_in_other_systems.description: Required large digital platforms to negotiate payment for Australian news content, addressing how AI-powered recommendation systems redirect attention and revenue away from journalism toward engagement-optimized content.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Google and Meta entered into payment agreements worth hundreds of millions with Australian news organizations. Demonstrated that governments can intervene in how AI-powered platforms distribute attention and value, though critics note the payments have benefited large media companies disproportionately.

issues.name: Autonomous weapons and lowering of violence thresholds
issues.description: AI-enabled autonomous weapons systems lower the political and psychological cost of deploying lethal force, making war easier to initiate and harder to contain.

reformers.name: International Committee for Robot Arms Control (ICRAC)
reformers.description: Interdisciplinary group of scientists, ethicists, and legal scholars who have produced detailed technical and policy analyses of autonomous weapons risks. ICRAC members have served as key experts in UN deliberations on lethal autonomous weapons systems and have shaped the technical framework through which these systems are evaluated.

reformers.name: Campaign to Stop Killer Robots
reformers.description: Global coalition of over 250 organizations in 70 countries working to preemptively ban fully autonomous weapons. Launched in 2013, the campaign has been the primary civil society force driving diplomatic attention to autonomous weapons at the UN Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons (CCW) and the UN General Assembly.

reformers.name: Stuart Russell
reformers.description: UC Berkeley computer science professor and author of the standard AI textbook who has become one of the most prominent AI researchers advocating against autonomous weapons. His short film 'Slaughterbots' vividly depicted the risks of autonomous lethal drones and has been shown in diplomatic settings to illustrate the stakes of inaction.

reformers.name: International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC)
reformers.description: The ICRC has formally called for new legally binding rules on autonomous weapons systems, arguing that existing international humanitarian law is insufficient to address weapons that select and engage targets without human control. Their position carries unique weight given their mandate as guardians of the Geneva Conventions.

reformers.name: Future of Life Institute (FLI)
reformers.description: Organization that coordinated the 2015 open letter signed by thousands of AI researchers calling for a ban on offensive autonomous weapons, and has since funded research on autonomous weapons governance. Their work has helped establish that the AI research community itself opposes unchecked autonomous weapons development.

legislation.name: UN General Assembly Resolution on Autonomous Weapons (2023)
legislation.description: First UN General Assembly resolution addressing autonomous weapons systems, calling on states to address challenges posed by autonomous weapons and requesting the Secretary-General to prepare a report on the topic. Passed with strong majority support, establishing autonomous weapons as a matter of global governance.

legislation.name: US Department of Defense Directive 3000.09 (Autonomy in Weapon Systems)
legislation.description: DoD policy requiring that autonomous and semi-autonomous weapon systems be designed to allow commanders and operators to exercise appropriate levels of human judgment over the use of force. Updated in 2023 to address AI-specific considerations but criticized for being a policy directive rather than binding law, with broad exception provisions.

legislation.name: US Replicator Initiative
legislation.description: Pentagon program to rapidly field thousands of autonomous systems across multiple domains. While framed as maintaining 'human-in-the-loop' control, the initiative's emphasis on speed, scale, and autonomous coordination represents a major acceleration of autonomous weapons deployment by the world's largest military.

legislation.name: Treaty on the Prohibition of Autonomous Weapons (proposed)
legislation.description: Proposed international treaty that would prohibit weapons systems that select and engage targets without meaningful human control. Supported by over 100 states in principle but blocked at the UN Convention on Certain Conventional Weapons by states with advanced autonomous weapons programs including the US, Russia, and Israel.

legislation.name: Israel's deployment of AI targeting systems in Gaza (Lavender/Gospel)
legislation.description: Reported AI systems used by the Israeli military to generate targeting recommendations at scale, with minimal human review time per target. Represents a de facto policy of AI-automated targeting in active conflict, lowering the effective threshold for lethal force application.

structurally_incentivized.name: Defense industry profit incentives
structurally_incentivized.description: Major defense contractors (Lockheed Martin, Raytheon, General Dynamics, BAE Systems) derive revenue from developing and selling increasingly autonomous weapons systems. The autonomous weapons market is projected to grow rapidly, creating powerful corporate incentives to develop and normalize these systems.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Defense contractors invest in autonomous weapons R&D because military customers demand capability advantages. Successful development creates pressure on rival nations to match capabilities, generating further demand. The industry profits from both the initial development and the resulting arms race.

structurally_incentivized.name: Geopolitical arms race dynamics
structurally_incentivized.description: No major military power can afford to forgo autonomous weapons development if rivals are pursuing it, creating a classic security dilemma where each nation's defensive investment appears threatening to others.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: The US develops autonomous systems because China is developing them; China develops them because the US is; Russia, India, Turkey, and others follow. Each actor's individually rational decision to invest creates a collectively dangerous acceleration that no single actor can stop unilaterally.

structurally_incentivized.name: Drone proliferation and cost collapse
structurally_incentivized.description: Commercial drone technology has made the hardware for autonomous weapons cheap and widely available. The barrier to entry for autonomous lethal systems is collapsing from billions to thousands of dollars, enabling non-state actors and smaller nations to field them.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Commercial off-the-shelf drones combined with open-source AI software lower autonomous weapons development costs by orders of magnitude. This democratization of lethal autonomous capability makes arms control agreements harder to enforce and verification nearly impossible.

structurally_incentivized.name: Casualty aversion in democracies
structurally_incentivized.description: Democratic publics are increasingly unwilling to accept military casualties. Autonomous weapons offer politicians the ability to project force without risking soldiers' lives, making military intervention politically easier to initiate.
structurally_incentivized.mechanism: Politicians face electoral consequences for military casualties but not for deploying autonomous systems. This asymmetry creates a structural incentive to prefer autonomous weapons, which in turn lowers the political threshold for initiating conflict.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Landmine ban (Ottawa Treaty)
reform_in_other_systems.description: The 1997 Convention on the Prohibition of Anti-Personnel Mines successfully stigmatized and largely eliminated a category of weapon through international treaty, despite opposition from major military powers including the US, Russia, and China. Frequently cited as a model for autonomous weapons governance.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: 164 states parties have joined the treaty. Landmine production and use declined dramatically, even among non-signatories, due to the stigmatization effect. Demonstrates that weapons categories can be effectively restricted through international norms even without universal participation.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Chemical Weapons Convention
reform_in_other_systems.description: International treaty banning the development, production, stockpiling, and use of chemical weapons, with a dedicated international organization (OPCW) for verification and enforcement. Demonstrates that weapons with indiscriminate effects can be prohibited through international law.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: 193 states parties; over 99% of declared chemical weapons stockpiles have been destroyed. The OPCW has conducted thousands of inspections. While violations have occurred (Syria), the norm against chemical weapons use remains strong and violations carry significant political costs.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Cluster Munitions Convention
reform_in_other_systems.description: Treaty banning cluster munitions, negotiated outside the UN framework when consensus-based CCW negotiations failed due to major power opposition — a model that autonomous weapons advocates are studying as a path forward when CCW deliberations are blocked.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: 111 states parties. Production and use declined significantly. Demonstrated that 'like-minded state' coalitions can create effective arms control frameworks outside of consensus-based UN processes, which is directly relevant to autonomous weapons governance.

reform_in_other_systems.name: Turkey's deployment of autonomous drones in Libya and Nagorno-Karabakh
reform_in_other_systems.description: Turkish Kargu-2 and Bayraktar TB2 drones operated with varying degrees of autonomy in conflicts in Libya (2020) and were pivotal in the Nagorno-Karabakh war, demonstrating how autonomous weapons change conflict dynamics and lower intervention thresholds for mid-tier powers.
reform_in_other_systems.outcome: Demonstrated that relatively inexpensive autonomous drone systems can decisively influence conflicts, accelerating global demand for similar capabilities. The Nagorno-Karabakh war became a case study in how autonomous weapons shift military balances rapidly and unpredictably.
